# Hierarchy Browser {#hierarchy-browser}

## Navigating a Massive Glycan Tree

The Hierarchy Browser lets you navigate the glycan structural
hierarchy --- a directed tree of subsumption relationships that
encodes which glycan structures are specialisations of which. With
58,628 glycan nodes connected by over 1.06 million edges, the full
graph is far too large to visualise at once or even to load into a
single network layout. This module solves that problem by letting you
explore one neighbourhood at a time through breadth-first search
traversals rooted at any glycan of your choosing. The result is a
focused, interactive subgraph that reveals local structure without
overwhelming you with the global complexity.

## Understanding Structural Subsumption

The hierarchy rests on the concept of structural subsumption, also
called structural containment. When we say parent_of(A, B), we mean
that glycan B's structure contains all of A's monosaccharide residues
and linkages, plus additional ones. A is a more general motif; B is a
more specific, more decorated structure. The inverse relation,
child_of(B, A), simply states that B is a specialisation of A. This
is not a classification taxonomy in the traditional sense --- it is a
lattice of structural inclusion, where each edge represents a
concrete chemical elaboration.

To make this concrete, consider the core pentasaccharide Man3GlcNAc2.
It is a parent of every complex-type N-glycan that extends it with
additional antennae, terminal sugars, and modifications. Each step
down the tree adds specific chemical detail:

```
Man3GlcNAc2  (parent)
 ├── Man3GlcNAc2·GlcNAc  (child, one antenna added)
 │    ├── Man3GlcNAc2·GlcNAc·Gal  (grandchild)
 │    │    └── Man3GlcNAc2·GlcNAc·Gal·Sia  (great-grandchild)
 │    └── ...
 ├── Man3GlcNAc2·GlcNAc·GlcNAc  (child, two antennae)
 └── ...
```

This tree structure is a key ingredient for the hierarchical
prediction tasks described in the Benchmark Dashboard
(Chapter \@ref(benchmarks)). The coarser tasks (superclass, class)
correspond to predictions high in this tree, where structures share
more commonality, while the finer tasks (family, exact ID) correspond
to predictions deep in the tree where structures diverge along many
possible branches.

## Setting Up a Traversal

The sidebar provides three controls that define each traversal. The
"Root Glycan ID" text input accepts a GlyTouCan accession (for
example, `G00051MO`) as the starting point for a breadth-first search.
If you do not know the accession, you can look it up on
[GlyTouCan](https://glytoucan.org/) or copy an ID from the Embedding
Explorer's hover labels. The module will validate the ID and display
an error message if it does not exist in the hierarchy graph.

The "Relation Type" radio button sets the traversal direction.
Choosing "parent_of" traverses downward from the root to its children,
showing structures that extend the root by adding chemical detail.
Choosing "child_of" traverses upward from the root to its parents,
revealing the more general motifs that the root elaborates upon. The
direction you choose depends on your question: downward exploration
answers "what more complex structures contain this motif?" while
upward exploration answers "what simpler motifs does this structure
build upon?"

The "Max Depth" slider, ranging from 1 to 5, limits how many hops the
BFS follows from the root. Because the hierarchy can branch
widely --- the average branching factor is roughly 4.2 --- keeping
depth at 2 or 3 is recommended for initial exploration. At depth 3
with a branching factor of 4, you could see up to 64 leaf nodes plus
all their ancestors, which is a manageable subgraph. Going to depth 5
could produce thousands of nodes and slow down the visualisation.

The traversal itself is a straightforward BFS that expands the
frontier one level at a time, tracking which nodes have been visited
and at what depth they were discovered:

```r
bfs_tree <- function(edges, root, max_depth, relation) {
  visited <- root
  frontier <- root
  depth_map <- setNames(0L, root)

  for (d in seq_len(max_depth)) {
    if (relation == "parent_of") {
      next_nodes <- edges$child[edges$parent %in% frontier]
    } else {
      next_nodes <- edges$parent[edges$child %in% frontier]
    }
    next_nodes <- setdiff(next_nodes, visited)
    if (length(next_nodes) == 0) break
    depth_map[next_nodes] <- d
    visited <- c(visited, next_nodes)
    frontier <- next_nodes
  }
  return(depth_map)
}
```

## The Interactive Tree Visualisation

The main panel renders the BFS result as an interactive network graph
using the visNetwork package with a top-down hierarchical layout. The
root node appears as a larger circle with a bold border, making it
easy to identify the starting point of your traversal. All other nodes
are coloured on a blue gradient by depth: depth 0 (the root) is dark
navy and depth 5 is light sky blue, so you can read the tree's
structure at a glance even before examining labels. Arrows point from
parent to child, encoding the direction of structural elaboration.

```r
visNetwork(nodes, edges) %>%
  visHierarchicalLayout(direction = "UD",
                        sortMethod = "directed") %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE,
             nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visInteraction(navigationButtons = TRUE)
```

The graph is fully interactive. Clicking a node selects it and
highlights its immediate neighbours, which is particularly useful for
tracing a single lineage through the tree. You can drag nodes to
rearrange the layout manually when the automatic positioning creates
overlaps. Scrolling zooms in and out, and navigation buttons in the
bottom-left corner provide fit-to-screen and directional panning. The
`nodesIdSelection` option adds a dropdown that lets you jump directly
to any node by its GlyTouCan ID, which is faster than scrolling
through a large tree.

## The Subtree Table

Below the network graph, a sortable data table lists every node in
the current BFS result. Each row shows the GlyTouCan accession
(rendered as a clickable link that opens the GlyTouCan entry in a new
tab), the depth from the root, and the number of direct children and
parents that node has in the full hierarchy. This count information is
valuable because it tells you how much further the tree extends beyond
the displayed subgraph --- a node with 50 children at the edge of
your traversal is a promising candidate for deeper exploration.

```r
DT::datatable(
  subtree_df,
  options = list(pageLength = 25, order = list(list(1, "asc"))),
  rownames = FALSE,
  escape = FALSE
)
```

The table is sorted by depth by default, which gives you a
level-by-level view of the subgraph. You can re-sort by children
count to find the most-branching nodes, or use the search box to
locate a specific accession.

## Three Ways to Explore

The most natural starting point is a downward exploration from a core
structure. Enter a core structure ID (such as the accession for
Man3GlcNAc2) in the root input, set the relation to "parent_of" with
max depth 3, and click "Update". The first level of the tree adds one
antenna, the second adds terminal sugars, and the third adds
sialylation or fucosylation. Clicking an interesting leaf node reveals
its ID, which you can paste into the Embedding Explorer
(Chapter \@ref(embedding-explorer)) to see where it falls in UMAP
space.

For the reverse perspective, enter a highly decorated glycan ID, set
the relation to "child_of" with max depth 4, and trace the path
upward. The tree converges toward increasingly general motifs, and the
broadest parent structure you reach is the glycan's structural class
anchor in the hierarchy. This upward view is especially useful for
understanding why a model might confuse two glycans --- if they share
a common parent at depth 2, their structures overlap substantially.

A third approach focuses on comparing siblings. Perform a downward
traversal from a shared parent at depth 1 and examine the sibling
nodes, which represent alternative extensions of the parent motif. You
can then check whether siblings appear in the same UMAP cluster or in
different ones, revealing whether the embedding model captures the
structural branching that the hierarchy encodes.

## Scale and Performance

The full hierarchy graph contains 58,628 glycan nodes, over 1,060,000
edges, a maximum observed depth of 12, and an average branching factor
of roughly 4.2. The module never loads the entire graph into the
visualisation --- the BFS traversal and depth limit ensure that only a
manageable subgraph is rendered. That said, if the BFS returns more
than roughly 500 nodes, the visNetwork graph may slow down noticeably,
particularly on machines without dedicated graphics hardware. Reducing
depth by one level or picking a more specific root usually brings the
node count back into a comfortable range.

## Practical Tips for Effective Browsing

Start shallow, at max depth 1 or 2, to orient yourself before
increasing depth. This lets you understand the local neighbourhood
before committing to a larger traversal. Right-clicking a node label
lets you copy the glycan accession for use in other modules or
external databases. The app supports Shiny bookmarking, so after
setting up an interesting view you can click "Bookmark" in the browser
to save the exact state --- root, relation, and depth --- as a
shareable URL. This is particularly useful when collaborating with
colleagues who want to see the same subgraph.

Finally, use the hierarchy depth of a glycan (from the subtree table)
alongside the Benchmark Dashboard (Chapter \@ref(benchmarks)) to
understand why prediction accuracy varies across hierarchical levels.
A glycan deep in the tree has many close structural neighbours, making
it harder to distinguish, which is exactly why the determination gap
described in the benchmark chapter exists.
